title: Enterprise GenAI Architecture
description: Multi-tenant platforms, access control, governance, observability, cost, and reliability.

questions.title: Design a secure enterprise RAG platform.
questions.scenario: A central enterprise AI platform team runs shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability. A recent review found that teams build isolated AI apps without common security, evaluation, and cost controls; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for secure enterprise RAG platform measured by platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate.
questions.expectedAnswer: secure enterprise RAG platform needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tenant documents, model calls, prompts, traces, safety events, and cost records, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to retrieving plausible but unsupported context. secure enterprise RAG platform addresses that risk through retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from tenant documents, model calls, prompts, traces, safety events, and cost records. Implement the change with Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

relatedQuestions.title: Why would an enterprise use a model gateway?

commonMistakes: Explaining secure enterprise RAG platform as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate.
Ignoring the main risk: retrieving plausible but unsupported context.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that secure enterprise RAG platform improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying secure enterprise RAG platform, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: A central enterprise AI platform team applied secure enterprise RAG platform in shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability. The baseline suffered from retrieving plausible but unsupported context, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, then evaluated the change against platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for secure enterprise RAG platform, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Why would an enterprise use a model gateway?
questions.scenario: A central enterprise AI platform team runs shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability. questions.expectedAnswer: Why would an enterprise use a model gateway belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. questions.title: How do you design multi-tenancy for GenAI platforms?
questions.scenario: A central enterprise AI platform team runs shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability. A recent review found that teams build isolated AI apps without common security, evaluation, and cost controls; the baseline fails on 20-35% of reviewed cases, so the team needs a reliable approach for design multi-tenancy for GenAI platforms measured by platform adoption, leakage tests, p95 latency, cost allocation, and eval gate pass rate.
questions.expectedAnswer: design multi-tenancy for GenAI platforms belongs in the architecture discussion because it affects ownership boundaries, controls, observability, and rollout. In shared GenAI platform for RAG apps, model gateways, governance, and observability, the practical approach is requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from tenant documents, model calls, prompts, traces, safety events, and cost records, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo.
